content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length 2⋅N2\cdot N2⋅N consisting of **exactly two** occurrences of each integer from 111 to NNN.

Find a permutation PPP of length NNN, such that, for each 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N:


If we delete **both** occurrences of PjP_jPj​ **for all** i<j≤Ni \lt j \leq Ni<j≤N from AAA without changing the order of remaining elements, there will be **atmost** ∣A∣2\frac{|A|}{2}2∣A∣​ elements between both occurrences of PiP_iPi​ in remaining AAA.
It is guaranteed that one such permutation always exists.

If multiple such permutations exist, you may print any.

Note that a permutation of length NNN consists of all integers from 111 to NNN exactly once.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a positive integer NNN — the length of the array AAA is 2⋅N2\cdot N2⋅N.
The second line of each test case contains 2⋅N2\cdot N2⋅N space-separated integers A1,A2,…,A2⋅NA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_{2\cdot N}A1​,A2​,…,A2⋅N​ representing the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, NNN space-separated integers denoting a valid permutation PPP.


It is guaranteed that one such permutation always exists.

If multiple such permutations exist, you may print any.
constraints: Each integer i (1 ≤ i ≤ N) appears exactly twice in the array A
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N. The second line contains 2·N space‑separated integers A1 … A2N, where each integer from 1 to N appears exactly twice.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated integers – a permutation P of {1,…,N} that satisfies the condition described in the statement.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we output the numbers in the order of their first appearance. The produced permutations satisfy the required distance condition.

solution.approach: Observe that the required condition is always satisfied if we output the numbers in the order of their first appearance in the given array. While scanning A from left to right we keep a boolean visited array. When we encounter a value x for the first time (visited[x] is false) we append x to the answer and mark visited[x] = true. After the scan we have exactly N distinct numbers, i.e. a valid permutation. The proof follows from the fact that after removing all numbers whose first occurrence is later than the current one, the remaining array contains exactly 2·i elements (i numbers each twice) and the two occurrences of the i‑th chosen number cannot be farther apart than i positions, which is ≤ (2·i)/2. The algorithm is linear in the size of the input.
solution.timeComplexity: O(2·N) per test case, i.e. O(N) overall. The total over all test cases is O(∑N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for the visited boolean array and the answer vector.
